sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=11, col=14).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 202, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'ACTION7': "Invalid - gives 'Unknown action' error", 'LEFT': 'Moves player left ~6 columns', 'MOUSE': 'Clicks position. Green blocks: collects but game over after 8-9 total clicks (persists across resets). Dots/purple blocks/platforms: adds 1 purple pixel to bottom bar. Bar clicks: adds 1 purple pixel.', 'RIGHT': 'Moves player right ~6 columns'}
- Object taxonomy: {'background': 'black with gray dots', 'dots': 'yellow/white dots at row 11 below purple blocks', 'green_blocks': 'light green 21-pixel blocks in 2x7 grid', 'player': 'blue+yellow small object', 'platforms': 'large sky blue regions', 'purple_blocks_top': '3 purple blocks at rows 7-10'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'Starts empty, accumulates 1 purple pixel per click. Resets on game over. Currently 15 pixels.'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Collect green blocks with MOUSE, move LEFT toward pink cross to complete level', 'level2': 'UNKNOWN - all attempts to collect 14 green blocks fail with game over after 8-9 clicks'}
- Level log: {'level1': 'Completed - collect greens with MOUSE, approach pink cross, LEFT to complete', 'level2': 'Multiple game overs. Green counter persists. Bar accumulates from clicks. Testing bar=14 hypothesis.'}
- Cross-level notes: Bar accumulates purple from ANY click. Green click counter persists across game overs. Level 1: collect greens, LEFT to pink cross. Level 2: unknown solution - green counter persists.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 151 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 3.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking a yellow dot didn't change anything - just added 1 to the bar (8→9). The dots remain unchanged.
I'm running out of obvious ideas. Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. RIGHT action - I've mostly tried LEFT
2. Collecting greens in a different order (bottom row first, alternating, etc.)
3. Moving the player to specific positions before clicking
4. Clicking the sky blue platforms
5. Some combination of actions
Wait - I just realized something important. In level 1, the solution was: collect greens, then LEFT to pink cross. The pink cross was a TARGET that the player needed to reach. What if in level 2, there's a similar target that I need to reach with the player?
Looking at the board: there are purple blocks at the top. What if I need to move the player UP to reach them? But there's no UP action...
Or what if the purple blocks ARE the targets, and I need to collect greens first, then click the purple blocks?
Actually, let me reconsider the bar mechanism. The bar fills from clicks. What if the bar is NOT a game over trigger but a PROGRESS bar? Like, I need to fill it to a certain level to complete the puzzle?
If the bar threshold is 12 for game over, maybe I need to fill it to exactly some value like 7 or 10?
Wait, let me think about this differently. What if:
- The bar represents "moves remaining" or "energy"
- Game over at 12 means I've used too many moves
- The goal is to collect all 14 greens with FEWER than 12 actions
But that's impossible since there are 14 greens...
Unless I can collect multiple greens with one action? Like, if I click a position that's shared between two greens?
Or what if I need to collect greens in a specific pattern that only requires 7 clicks? Like, clicking one green causes adjacent ones to be collected too?
Let me try clicking a green that's between two others and see if it collects multiple.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the green blocks more carefully. They're in a 2x7 grid. What if clicking one row causes the other row to also be collected? Let me check if clicking a top-row green also removes the corresponding bottom-row green.
Actually, I already tested this - when I collected 7 top-row greens, only those 7 were removed, not the bottom 7.
Hmm, let me try a completely different approach. What if the solution involves RIGHT instead of LEFT? Or what if I need to move the player to the right side first?
Let me try: RIGHT, then collect some greens, then see if something different happens.
Actually, I just thought of something else. What if I need to fill the bar to exactly 7 (matching the 7 greens per row)? Let me try:
1. Game over to reset bar to 0
2. Collect 7 greens (bar 0→7)
3. Click something specific to trigger completion
Or what if the bar needs to reach exactly 7 and I need to click non-green things to reach 7 without collecting all greens?
Let me try yet another approach: what if I need to click the purple blocks first (3 clicks, bar=3), then collect 4 greens (bar=7)? Total bar=7.
Actually, I realize I should try something completely different. Let me try RIGHT to move the player to the right side and see if there's something there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Dot clicks just add to bar, no state change.
# Let me try RIGHT to move player to right side - maybe there's something there.
# Also try collecting greens in alternating pattern.

# First trigger game over to reset, then try a fresh approach
# Game over at bar=12, current bar=9. Need 3 more clicks to trigger.

# Click 3 more greens to trigger game over
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
green_centers = []
for g in greens:
    rows = [p[0] for p in g['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in g['boundary']]
    r = (min(rows) + max(rows)) // 2
    c = (min(cols) + max(cols)) // 2
    green_centers.append((r, c))
green_centers.sort()

# Click 3 greens to trigger game over
for i, (r, c) in enumerate(green_centers[:3]):
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c}])
    if last_action_result.get('game_over'):
        print(f"Game over after click {i+1}")
        break

# Now try RIGHT on fresh board
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"After reset: {len(greens)} greens")

# Try RIGHT
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
player = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
if player:
    p = player[0]
    print(f"Player after RIGHT: boundary={p['boundary']}")
bar = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p' and n['boundary'][0][0] == 63]
print(f"Bar: {[n['pixels'] for n in bar]}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}, LC: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"ACTION7": "Invalid", "LEFT": "Moves player left ~6 columns, adds 1 to bar", "MOUSE": "Green: collects + adds 1 to bar. Other: adds 1 to bar. Game over at bar=12.", "RIGHT": "Moves player right ~6 columns, adds 1 to bar"}, "cross_level_notes": "Bar threshold=12. Dot clicks don't change state. Testing RIGHT.", "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "Resets on game over. Threshold=12."}, "level_log": {"level2": "Testing RIGHT after game over reset."}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Collect greens, LEFT to pink cross"}}, "level_tier": {"current_plan": "Trigger game over, try RIGHT on fresh board", "failed_probes": ["Collect all 14 greens", "Collect 7 then LEFT", "Purple block clicks", "Dot clicks", "Bar filling"], "goal_model": "Unknown - trying RIGHT movement", "recent_findings": ["Dot clicks just add to bar", "7 greens remain after collecting top row"], "world_model": "7 greens (bottom row), bar=9, player on left"}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Game over after click 1
After reset: 18 greens
Bar: [10]
Done: False, LC: False
